When at **max adults**, Edmonto Regalis will mock stomp and be vocally aggressive towards all **adult apex** carnivores until the carnivore attempts to flee away from the Edmonto Regalis and its herd.  After **3 mock stomps** or **3 threat calls**, if the adult apex carnivores do not attempt to move away or flee from the Edmonto or its herd, they may become **combat aggressive** and chase carnivores out of the POI. 
- When **solo** or in an Edmontosaurus Regalis only herd, will be **vocally aggressive** to adult sub-apex or below carnivores.  After **3 threat calls**, if the carnivores do not attempt to move away or flee from the Edmonto or its herd, they may become **combat aggressive** and chase carnivores out of POI. 
- When in a **mixed herd**, adult Edmontosaurus Regalis are vocally aggressive to adult mid-tier or below carnivores.  After **3 threat calls**, if the carnivores do not attempt to move away or flee from the Edmonto or its herd, they may become **combat aggressive** and chase carnivores out of POI. 
- When in a **mixed herd** with an **adult** Edmontosaurus Annectens, adult Edmontosaurus Regalis are unbothered by all carnivores, unless there is an attempt to initiate combat by bite or claw attack. 
-

**Scavenger Aggression:**
- When **solo** or in an **adult-only herd**, adult Edmontosaurus Regalis are unbothered by all scavengers. 
- When **juvi/adol hadrosaurs** are present, adult Edmontosaurus Regalis will be vocally aggressive towards grouped scavengers.  They will ignore solo scavengers.
**Scavenger Low Pounce/Latch Aggression**
- When pounced on by scavengers, may be unbothered. If bothered by the latched on scavengers, must threat call **three** times or shake **three** times before attacking the scavengers. In this scenario, if the scavengers cannot hunt this tier of dinosaur they must **not fight** back and instead must **flee**.
*Note: The latched on scavengers cannot attack/initiate combat first if it would break their profile's hunting restrictions/behaviors.*

**Territory Size:** 
  Medium - Needs at least 3 adult members to claim.  
  Large - Needs 4 adult members to claim. [*Please see Territory Rules for mix-herd options.*](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1mnbJnMb6H9FuCYLmMM28ncJT-Ar6sIWk4qCUa89VwgI/edit?usp=sharing)

**Leadership Type:**  
  Mated Pair - Dual Leadership. 
-

**Optional Nesting Behavior:**
- __Season__: Wet
- __Preferred Nesting Location(s)__: 
> **Gondwa**: Burned Forest,Rainbow Hills, Savanna Grassland 
> **Panjura**: Cliffside Retreat, Threehorn Grassland, Travelers Basin 
- __Preferred Skins/Coloration(s)__: Regalis,like Edmontosaurus Annectens, had similar interests when it came to their colorations and mimicked the same color preferences and averseness. Males would adorn themselves with bright colors along their bodies and their nose, while solid shades and melanistic tones were often avoided. 
> Unlike their larger counterparts, Edmontosaurus Regalis had a softer approach towards mating seasons.  A single mated pair would often lead the herd even after nesting seasons have ended.  Males would be the ones to make nests for their mates and would stay with a watchful eye while females would forage for food.  During nesting seasons, the leading mated pair would often look after other herd members kin. They would also sometimes share nesting grounds with Annectens and kept a watchful eye over their kin, and took in any orphaned young.
-

**Optional Behavior:** 
  They are not afraid to cross rivers or other bodies of water if it means herd safety and food.  Unlike its larger counterpart, they often spend many days at one location as long as food is bountiful, only moving when necessary.  Although they share their love and protection for their young, if the parents do not think they are fit to raise them they will seek out an Annectens herd to care for them.  While males all feel protective of the herd, if more than one is in the herd they will often quarrel with each other to prove who's better.  The females stick close to their young, even caring for the young of other cows in the herd.  Like their larger counterparts, they rest during the nights in a similar fashion.

-
**Mandatory Behavior: **
○ __Survivor:__ **Adolescent and below** Yunnanosaurus must flee from adult mid-tier and above carnivores. May defend themself if cornered or exhausted.

- Note: If semi-aquatic or aquatic carnivores are within line of sight or visible in the water, Yunnanosaurus does not have to cross and may fight for their life.

**Scavenger Aggression:**
- Yunnanosaurus must be **vocally aggressive** towards scavengers. If after three threat calls, the scavengers do not attempt to flee, Yunnanosaurus *may* become **combat aggressive** until the scavengers flee territory or perish.

**Scavenger High Pounce/Latch Aggression**
- When pounced on by scavengers, they must be **bothered** and must immediately shake-off and **initiate combat** on the latched scavengers. In this scenario, if the scavengers cannot hunt this tier of dinosaur they **must not** fight back and instead **must flee**.

-

> **Panjura**: Deep Lake, Lakeside Meadow, Two Brother’s Hollow, Threehorn Grasslands

- __Preferred Skins/Coloration(s):__ Males adorned themselves in bright, flashy patterns and females were seen with patches of reds, blues, or greens, but mostly had darker colors.
> Brave and tenacious, the male pro-sauropods gathered around in leks to impress the females in a series of show-downs.  These included sound intimidation, kicks, beating their belly, and posing firm with his long back legs.  This process continued until one male remained, granting them the rights to the harem.  The rest of the defeated males were either chased out or forced to become a subordinate – a fate decided upon by the winner.  Females built nests and took care of their offspring, while the lead male protected the herd from any hazard and maintained their dominance from other foreign Yunnanosaurus or young males within the herd.

**Optional Behavior:**
    The Yunnanosaurus are very tenacious dinosaurs and do not like backing down when confronted, even when it may leave their offspring in danger.  They are vocal from the times between dawn and noon as they wander between food source to food source to fill their insatiable appetite.  Male Yunnanosaurus can often be found sporting brighter colors and sparring with other males in the herd for their place in leadership, beating on their stomachs as a form of intimidation.

- __Preferred Skins/Coloration(s)__: Female Therizinosaurs would often display brighter colors along their tail feathers and often had dark colors transition to white at the tips. > Female Therizinosaurus were known to be far more territorial and aggressive than males.  They would infrequently find suitable male mates which resulted in duo mares often courting each other.  Though aggressive to males, females would exhibit very caring and emotionally in-tuned behaviors.  Acts of preening and sharing meals with one another were ways that these pairs could bond.  Though they couldn’t lay eggs, female pairs would often adopt orphaned Therizinosaurus and were also known to adopt Citipati and Struthiomimus young, treating them no differently than their own kin.  
-

**Optional Behavior:**
If confronted and threatened by a carnivore, Therizinosaurus will not back down easily and tends to stand its ground even against threatening Apex carnivores.  When grouped with a mate and offspring, Therizinosaurus is often seen taking "shifts" between patrolling the territory and watching over the offspring.  Therizinosaurus will also choose to rest and preside over their food sources within their territory, especially when with offspring.  They often graze on their claimed food sources, keeping an eye out for potential carnivores that may prey on their young or even herbivores that may aim to consume all their food.  Therizinosaurus tends to be most active from dawn-to-dusk, choosing not to roam or migrate once night-time arrives.

○ __Hedge of Thorns:__ When **adolescent and below** stegosaurids are present, adult Stegosaurus **must not flee** from combat unless gravely injured and death is likely [~15% hp or less].
*note: stegosaurids include; Stegosaurus, Miragaia and Kentrosaurus*
○ __Survivor:__ Adolescent and below Stegosaurus **must flee** from adult mid-tier and above carnivores. May defend themself if cornered or exhausted.
-

**Carnivore Aggression:**
- When **solo**, Stegosaurus are **fearful** of and **must flee from** adult sub-apex carnivore groups, and adult apex carnivores. May defend themself if cornered or exhausted.
- When in herd of 3 **or more adults,** adult Stegosaurus **must** be **vocally aggressive** to approaching adult sub-apex carnivores. If after **3 threat calls** or **3 mock attacks**, the carnivores do not attempt to flee from Stegosaurus and its herd, they may become **combat aggressive** and chase the carnivores **until** they flee.
*Note: Once Stegosaurus successfully defends themselves or their herd, Stegosaurus must not pursue fleeing carnivores. *

**Scavenger Aggression:**
- Stegosaurus **must** be **vocally aggressive** towards scavengers. If after **3 threat calls** or **3 mock attacks**, the scavengers do not attempt to flee from Stegosaurus and its herd, they may become **combat aggressive** and chase the scavengers **until** they flee.

**Scavenger High Pounce/Latch Aggression**
- When pounced on by scavengers, they must be **bothered** and must immediately shake-off and **initiate combat** on the latched scavengers. In this scenario, if the scavengers cannot hunt this tier of dinosaur they **must not** fight back and instead **must flee**.
*Note: The latched on scavengers **must not** initiate combat first if it would break their profile's hunting restrictions/behaviors.*
-

**Territory Size:**
> Medium - Needs at least 3 adult members to claim.  
> Large - Needs 4 adult members to claim. 
> [*Please see Territory Rules for mix-herd options.*](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1mnbJnMb6H9FuCYLmMM28ncJT-Ar6sIWk4qCUa89VwgI/edit?usp=sharing)

**Leadership Type:**
  Patriarchal - Male Dominant.

**Optional Nesting Behavior:**
- __Season__: Dry
- __Preferred Nesting Location(s)__: 
> **Gondwa**: Green Valley (lower), Snake Gully, Green hills
> **Panjura**: Hilltop Haven, the Cascades [Haven], Deep Lake
> **Riparia**: Hollow Hills, Black Fern Hills, Redwoods
- __Preferred Skins/Coloration(s)__: Males showed bright colors on their plates to impress their mates and to intimidate other males who might want to move in on their nests.  Females featured more natural colors in order to blend in with the environment.
> Stegosaurus instinctually came from far and wide to nest together and their rituals included shaking their bodies to make the colors on their plates appear to move as well as displaying their ability to protect their young from predators and other male stegosaurus.
> If two males were interested in a female, they might challenge one another by hitting each other with their tails until one submitted.  Once the baby stegosaurus reached adolescence, the mother opted to move her family around away from the nest.  Stegosaurus families were independent, but they still enjoyed nesting in close proximity to other Stegosaurids.

**Optional Behavior:**
 It's often rare to see Stegosaurus alone as they choose to remain in large groups with other Stegosaurids.  Stegosaurus spends most of its day grazing, napping, or roaming its territory.  The males will often duel with each other for ownership of the herd, the leader of the herd will wound the calves of other males to assert dominance.

*Note: The latched on scavengers **must not** initiate combat first if it would break their profile's hunting restrictions/behaviors.*

> As the seasons change and the weather grows cold, mature male Peloroplites gather in groups to spar. The larger and stronger the male is, the more fights he wins, and the better his chances of catching the eye of a female. While these fights rarely go to the death, any injuries the males sustain can spell disaster in the upcoming winter. Males will often spend time sizing one another up and showing off their power through tail slams and bellows before trading blows, in the attempt to scare away their opponents without risking injury. 
-

**Optional Behavior:**
  Peloroplites, despite their size and fearsome spikes, are a reclusive species, rarely venturing from the forests they call home. Their tough beaks allow them to forage on tough vegetation and roots that other species tend to avoid, which lets them coexist peacefully with most herbivores. Peloroplites also do not seem to mind the presence of smaller carnivores such as Deinonychus or Rhamphorhynchus, and may allow the little carnivores to perch on their backs and pick off parasites. It is only during the nesting season, with young to defend, that these ankylosaurs become aggressive.

- *Note sub apex and above Hadrosaurs include: Barsboldia, Edmontosaurus Annectens, Edmontosaurus Regalis, and Parasaurolophus*
○ __Survivor__: **Adolescent and below** Parasaurolophus must flee from adult **mid-tier and above** carnivores. *May* defend themself if cornered or exhausted.

- When in an **adult-only herd**, Parasaurolophus are **unbothered** by **mid-tier and below** carnivores. Adult Parasaurolophus **must** be **vocally aggressive** towards *approaching* adult apex carnivores.  If after **3 threat calls**, the carnivores **do not** attempt to move away or flee from the Parasaurolophus and its herd, Parasaurolophus *may* become **combat aggressive** until the carnivores flee the territory or perish.
-

**Scavenger Aggression:**

If after **3 threat calls**, the scavengers do not attempt to move away or flee from the Parasaurolophus and its herd, Parasaurolophus **may** become combat aggressive until the scavengers flee.

**Scavenger Low Pounce/Latch Aggression**
- When pounced on by scavengers, may be unbothered. If bothered by the latched on scavengers, they must threat call **three** times or shake **three** times before attacking the scavengers. In this scenario, if the scavengers cannot hunt this tier of dinosaur they **must not** fight back and instead **must flee**.

-

**Leadership Type:**
   Leading Bull - Male Dominant. 

**Optional Nesting Behavior:**
- __Season__: Wet
- __Preferred Nesting Location(s)__:

> **Riparia**: Wollemi Forest, Black Fern Hills and Hollow Hills
> **Panjura**: Green Plateau, Pleasant Hill Haven, Riverland Peak 
- __Preferred Skins/Coloration(s)__: 
During nesting seasons, male paras would frequently be adorned with brightly colored crests and unique patterns and colors on their bodies.  Females would sometimes remain neutral colored, however, they sometimes also adorned brightly colored crests.
Parasaurolophus, like many hadrosaurs, could be seen as picky during their nesting season.  Females typically showed interest in strong bulls and males with the largest crest were typically seen as the most viable candidate for mating.  Typical courting rituals would involve two paras performing a synchronized dance and using their crests almost like an instrument during the dances.  These mating pairs were most commonly only temporary liaisons but, occasionally, certain pairs would stick with one another for life. Females would often create and tend to communal nesting grounds, while males would leave to forage for food.  Females took shifts watching over young, which, in turn, allowed mothers to rest.  Hatchlings and juveniles would not stray far from their parents but adolescents were more rowdy and play fighting and bickering was common amongst hot-headed parasaurolophus.
-

**Optional Behavior: **
  Parasaurolophus, despite their quite timid and careful character, form big and, definitely dangerous, herds that consist of numerous individuals of both genders.  These herbivores show an easy going and gentle nature and are not easily threatened by large carnivores.  They are often seen with their own offspring and as well as the young of different hadrosaur herbivore species.  Strict hierarchy within the herd is the main factor keeping the group together and safe - males often take on the role of protectors while females, more often than not, run from danger and lead their offspring and younger members to safety.  Parasaurolophus are diurnal herbivores - active during the day to migrate, fill on food, and lead the herd to water sources.  Bulls are often seen fighting, especially during the nesting season.  At night, however, they show more cautious behavior, often seeking shelter and staying quiet to avoid potential nocturnal predators that are prowling around looking for an unsuspecting meal.

○ __Territorial__: When in their **ceratopsian herd's claimed territory**, adult Pachyrhinosaurus must be **vocally aggressive** to other adult sub-apex ceratopsians not in their group.  If after 3 threat calls, the ceratopsians do not attempt to flee, adult Pachyrhinosaurus *may* become **combat aggressive** until the ceratopsians flee the territory or perish. If fearful or unwilling to engage other sub-apex ceratopsians not in their herd, Pachyrhinosaurus **must immediately leave** the territory. 
○ __Brash Babies__: **Adolescent and below** Pachyrhinosaurus *may* be **vocally aggressive** to adult scavengers that are around their group. If after **3 threat calls** the scavengers do not attempt to flee away from the Pachyrhinosaurus or its herd, Pachyrhinosaurus *may* become **combat aggressive** but must not pursue the fleeing scavengers. 
○ __Survivor__: **Adolescent and below** Pachyrhinosaurus **must flee** from adult mid-tier and above carnivores. May defend themself if cornered or exhausted.

**Ceratopsian Combat:**
Ceratopsian herds (those made up of Albertaceratops, Eotriceratops, Nasutoceratops, Pachyrhinosaurus, Pentaceratops, or Styracosaurus) often engage in herbi-on-herbi combat with each other, and may initiate combat __when specifically outlined in their profile mandatory behaviors.__  ﻿Ceratopsians may defend their herd from other ceratopsians.
- Forager-tier herbivores grouped with __only__ Ceratopsians in a mixed herd are still classified as a Ceratopsian Herd. When forager-tier herbivores, such as Camptosaurus, Dryosaurus,  Psittacosaurus, and Struthiomimus are in those herds, the forager-tier dinosaurs **must not** engage in Ceratopsian Herd vs Ceratopsian Herd combat. Instead, the forager-tiers **must flee** from this specific Ceratopsian combat.
- If a Ceratopsian(s) is in a mixed-herd with any low-tier and above herbivore that is not a Ceratopsian(s), they are **not** classified as a Ceratopsian Herd. [*See Pack & Herd Rules 6.(C)(i)*](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1Xw4QL-RWispl_cPW3qCjBFKPsjfrNSfSqHjCJ3WWsP8/edit?usp=sharing)

**Carnivore Aggression:**
- When **solo**, adult Pachyrhinosaurus **must** be **vocally aggressive** towards *approaching* adult mid-tier and below carnivores. If after **3 threat calls**, the carnivores do not attempt to flee away from the Pachyrhinosaurus, Pachyrhinosaurus may become combat aggressive until the carnivores flee the territory or perish.
- When **solo**, adult Pachyrhinosaurus are **fearful of** and **must flee** from adult sub-apex and adult apex carnivores. May defend themself if cornered or exhausted.
- When in a **herd**, adult Pachyrhinosaurus *may* be **vocally aggressive** to adult apex and below carnivores. If after **3 threat calls**, the carnivores do not attempt to flee from Pachyrhinosaurus, Pachyrhinosaurus *may* become **combat aggressive** until the carnivores flee the territory or perish.
-

**Scavenger Aggression:**
- When in an **adult-only herd**, Pachyrhinosaurus are unbothered by scavengers
- When **solo** or when **adolescent and below** herbivores are present,  adult Pachyrhinosaurus **must** be **vocally aggressive** towards *approaching* scavenger *groups*. If after **3 threat calls**, the scavengers do not attempt to move away or flee from the Pachyrhinosaurus and its herd, Pachyrhinosaurus may become combat aggressive until the scavengers flee.

**Scavenger Moderate Pounce/Latch Aggression**
- When pounced on by scavengers, may be unbothered. If bothered by the latched on scavengers, they must threat call **two** times or shake **two** times before attacking the scavengers. In this scenario, if the scavengers cannot hunt this tier of dinosaur they **must not** fight back and instead **must flee**.
*Note: The latched on scavengers **must not** initiate combat first if it would break their profile's hunting restrictions/behaviors.*

**Territory Size:**
    Medium - Needs at least 3 adult members to claim. [*Please see Territory Rules for mix-herd options.*](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1mnbJnMb6H9FuCYLmMM28ncJT-Ar6sIWk4qCUa89VwgI/edit?usp=sharing)

**Leadership Type:**
       Leading Bull - Male Dominant.
-

**Optional Nesting Behavior:**
- __Season__: Winter
- __Preferred Nesting Location(s)__: 
> **Gondwa:** Mudflats, Ripple Beach, Birchwoods
> **Panjura:** The Mire, Threehorn Grasslands, Travelers Basin
> **Riparia:** Mudflats, Redwoods, Tallbrush Coasts
- __Preferred Skins/Coloration(s)__: Male Pachyrhinosaurus adorned themselves with brightly coloured crests and the same colors would flow down to their backside and tails. Cooler hues, like blues and purples, were popular amongst males and signified they were healthy. These color tones also matched the chilly environments in which they would nest. These bright colors also served as beacons for lost young through thick snow storms.
> During nesting season, males either fought for breeding rights within the herd or left to find a suitable partner to start their own herd. Males attracted a partner by flaunting their nasal bosses. Females would pay attention to battle scars that males possessed on their nose and took notice of the wear and tear of their nasal bosses to deem them as a fit candidate to protect their young. Juvenile Pachyrhinosaurus were notably temperamental and fights between siblings were frequent, and sometimes even dangerous. While head-strong, they were still naive and would often pick fights with other,  sometimes older, Pachyrhinosaurus, which would sometimes lead to their untimely demise. 
-
**Optional Behavior:**
       Pachyrhinosaurus usually sought refuge in tidal flats, floodplains, wooded swamps, and mountainous regions, preferring to travel between these environments to find plenty of ferns and low hanging fruits for food. When attacked or being hunted by carnivorous packs, Pachyrhinosaurus created a circle of protection, ushering their young and sometimes female counterparts within the center as they warded off predators with mock charges and head swings. Due to their instincts to protect members of their ceratopsian species, old, weaker bulls often sacrificed themselves to these packs in dire situations, which allowed the rest of the herd to flee to safety. Fights amongst young bulls were common as these young males attempted to climb the herd's hierarchy, eventually challenging the oldest bull for leadership of the herd.

- __Preferred Skin Colour__: Males stuck with blues and whites to assert dominance by being flashy.  Females tended to blend in with the environment to protect themselves and their kids, which tended to be more brown and black.
> Iguanodons are incredibly protective of their young unless they are gravely injured and close to death, then they will flee the immediate area, even leaving their own children behind. Juvis/Adols always remained near their parents unless they were hiding from a fight, during which they chose to hide in among bushes or outright flee the area.  Bulls were aggressive when it came to courting. They would broadcast, along with stomping their feet, to assert dominance when attempting to court a female.  Female Iggys tended to be docile towards the male, but could become aggressive if the male overstepped.  If either male/female came upon a lone juvi/adol, they would happily accept them into the herd (as long as they were herd tolerant).
-

**Optional Behavior:** 
  Iguanodon are social creatures.  As they travel to resources, they will lovingly call out to passing herbivores and will take in any herbivore juvenile/adolescent that fits within the group.  They enjoy midday and have low activity at dusk and night.  When traveling with children, they will stick to the forests in order for the kids to have a place to hide in case of an attack.  Bulls are more aggressive than females and will protect the herd, while the cows stay with the young.  When attacked, juvi/adol Iguanodons will huddle together to stay safe.  If there are no other children, the juvi/adol will stick close to its parent for protection.

-

**Carnivore Aggression:**
- When in a herd with no adult apex maniraptoriformes present, adult Gigantoraptor **must** be **vocally aggressive** towards solo sub-apex canrivores, and adult mid-tier and below carnivores. If after **3 threat calls**, the carnivores do not attempt to move away or flee from the Gigantoraptor and its herd, Gigantoraptor *may* become **combat aggressive** and pursue fleeing carnivores until they flee the territory or perish.
- When in a herd with adult apex maniraptoriformes are present, adult Gigantoraptor **must** be **vocally aggressive** to all adult mid-tier and above carnivores.  If after **3 threat calls**, the carnivores do not attempt to move away or flee from the Gigantoraptor and its herd, Gigantoraptor *may* become **combat aggressive** and pursue fleeing carnivores until they flee the territory or perish.
*Note: Apex maniraptoriformes include: Deinocheirus and Therizinosaurus.*
- When **adolescent and below** herbivores are present, adult Gigantoraptor **must** be **vocally aggressive** to all *approaching* adult carnivores.  If after **3 threat calls** the carnivores do not attempt to move away or flee Gigantoraptor and its herd, Gigantoraptor *may* become **combat aggressive** until the carnivores flee .
-

**Scavenger Aggression:**
- Adult Gigantoraptor **must** be **vocally aggressive** towards all adult scavengers no matter their pack size.  If after three threat calls the scavengers do not attempt to flee from the Gigantoraptor or its herd, Gigantoraptor *may* become **combat aggressive** and pursue fleeing scavengers until they flee.

**Scavenger High Pounce/Latch Aggression**
- When pounced on by scavengers, they must be **bothered** and must immediately shake-off and **initiate combat** on the latched scavengers. In this scenario, if the scavengers cannot hunt this tier of dinosaur they **must not** fight back and instead **must flee**.
*Note: The latched on scavengers **must not** initiate combat first if it would break their profile's hunting restrictions/behaviors.*

**Territory Size:** 
> Medium - Needs at least 3 adult members to claim. 
> [*Please see Territory Rules for mix-herd options.*](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1mnbJnMb6H9FuCYLmMM28ncJT-Ar6sIWk4qCUa89VwgI/edit)

**Leadership Type:**
   Independent - Will think of their own personal benefit before others.  
-

**Optional Nesting Behavior:**
- __Season__: Winter
- __Preferred Nesting Locations__:
> **Gondwa**: Burned Forest, Stego Mountains, Wilderness Peak
> **Panjura**: Cliffside Retreat, Snake Valley, The Mire
- __Preferred Skins/Coloration(s)__: Male Gigantoraptor molted their feathers and presented flashy colors around the tips of their wings as well as the feathers that covered their face.  Their bills also lightened in shade during nesting seasons, which allowed them to be more recognizable to potential offspring.  Females molted their feathers to match with compatible mating partners but kept the shades as monotone as possible so they could hide in plain sight to protect their eggs.
> During the cold winter season, the sound of clicking beaks could be heard from within the forest accompanied by the small chirps of newly hatched young, who were under the watchful eye of this large avian bird.  The Gigantoraptor was a very attentive parent and the females could be found resting on clutches of eggs as well as freshly hatched young.  Meanwhile, the males were out patrolling the nesting area and warding off any possible threats to their nests.  The females, who were known to usher their hatchlings underneath them to keep them safe, would fiercely defend against anything that wandered too close to their precious nesting grounds.  Males often sparred with one another and showed off their bright coloration in a careful dance hoping to win the eye of a potential mate and being chosen to bring forth new generations into the world.
-

**Optional Behavior:**
   Gigantoraptors wasted little time in finding food and rarely discriminated between sources, eating just about anything they could forage and leaving nothing behind.  They needed constant fuel to stay warm because their feathers were better suited for keeping their eggs warm than providing much insulation for themselves.  The plumage, particularly on their arms, was used for display as well as scaring away would-be egg thieves, or drawing the attention of a potential mate.  These giant birds stayed broody long after their offspring hatched, which caused them to adopt feathered hatchlings from other species that were left behind by their parents, regardless if they are independent.  When Gigantoraptors weren’t raising hatchlings, they sought the company of other avian dinosaurs to satisfy their high social needs.
``` ```

> *Divine Beast Ampelosaurus*
> 5 Hours Passive Growth Time
> 5500 Combat Weight 

**Lifestyle:**
    Terrestrial

**Group Limit**: 
     2 adults, 3 offspring (below sub-adult)

**Herd Tolerance**: 
> Amargasaurus, Camptosaurus, Citipati, Iguanodon, Lambeosaurus, Lurdusaurus, Muttaburrasaurus, Pachycephalosaurus, Psittacosaurus, Struthiomimus, Styracosaurus, Tenontosaurus, and Yunnanosaurus.
> *Please see the [Pack & Herd Rules](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1Xw4QL-RWispl_cPW3qCjBFKPsjfrNSfSqHjCJ3WWsP8/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.evs5v6kmvhm) for mix-herd limits.*
-

**Mandatory Behavior**:
○  __Tactical Retreat__:  If **solo** Garrigatitan are next to a crossable body of water **when combat starts** against **grouped adult** apex carnivores, they **must** cross that body of water instead of engaging in combat.
- *Note: If semi-aquatic or aquatic carnivores are within line of sight or visible in the water, Garrigatitan does not have to cross and may fight for their life.*
○  __Discriminate Guardian__:  Adult Garrigatitan **must not** defend grouped adolescent and below non-sauropod herbivores. 
○  __Survivor__:  **Adolescent and below** Garrigatitan **must flee** from adult sub-apex apex and above carnivores. May defend themself if cornered or exhausted.
-

**Carnivore Aggression**:
- When **solo**, adult Garrigatitan **must** be **vocally aggressive** to approaching **grouped adult sub-apex** and **solo adult apex carnivores**.  If after 3 threat calls the carnivores do not attempt to flee, Garrigatitan *may* become combat aggressive and chase fleeing carnivores until they leave the territory or perish.
- When **solo**, adult Garrigatitan are **wary** of **grouped** adult apex carnivores. If attacked, and not near a safe, crossable body of water, Garrigatitan may pursue retreating adult apex carnivores in self-defense until they leave the territory or perish.
- When in a **herd** and ** adolescent and below sauropods** are present, Garrigatitan **must** be **vocally aggressive** to all approaching **grouped low-tier and above** carnivores. If after 3 threat calls, the carnivores do not attempt to flee away from the Garrigatitan or its herd, Garrigatitan *may* become combat aggressive and chase fleeing carnivores until they leave the territory or perish.
- When in a **mix-herd**, adult Garrigatitan are **unbothered** by **grouped** adult low-tier and **solo** mid-tier carnivores unless there is an attempt to initiate combat by the carnivore(s). If the carnivore attempts to flee away from the Garrigatitan and its herd, Garrigatitan **must not** pursue them.
- When in a **mix-herd**, Garrigatitan **must** be **vocally aggressive** to **grouped** adult mid-tier carnivores and above. If after 3 threat calls the carnivores do not attempt to flee away from the Garrigatitan or its herd, Garrigatitan *may* become combat aggressive and chase the fleeing carnivores until they leave the territory or perish.

- When in an **adult only** sauropod herd, adult Garrigatitan **must** be **unbothered** by all carnivores unless there is an attempt to initiate combat by the carnivore. If the carnivore attempts to flee away from the Garrigatitan and its herd, they **must not** pursue them.
- *Note: Sauropods include - Amargasaurus, Garrigatitan, and Yunnanosaurus.*

**Scavenger Aggression**:
- When in a **herd** and ** adolescent and below sauropods** are present, Garrigatitan **must** be **vocally aggressive** to all approaching **grouped scavengers**. If after 3 threat calls, the carnivores do not attempt to flee away from the Garrigatitan or its herd, Garrigatitan *may* become combat aggressive until the scavengers flee from Garrigatitan and its herd.

**Scavenger Low Pounce/Latch Aggression**
- When pounced on by scavengers, may be unbothered. If bothered by the latched on scavengers, they must threat call **three** times or shake **three** times before attacking the scavengers. In this scenario, if the scavengers cannot hunt this tier of dinosaur they **must not** fight back and instead **must flee**.
*Note: The latched on scavengers **must not** initiate combat first if it would break their profile's hunting restrictions/behaviors.*
-

**Territory Size**:
> Large - Can claim as a solo adult or Garrigatitan group. [*Please see Territory Rules for mix-herd options.*](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1mnbJnMb6H9FuCYLmMM28ncJT-Ar6sIWk4qCUa89VwgI/edit?usp=sharing)

**Leadership Type**:
Patriarchal - Bull Leadership.
-

**Optional Nesting Behavior:**
- __Season__: Dry 
- __Preferred Nesting Location(s)__:
> **Gondwa**: Burned Forest, Hoodoo Expanse, Hotsprings 
> **Panjura**: Aperture Pass, Grassland Lake, Sleeping Giant 
- __Preferred Skin(s)/Coloration(s)__: Male Garrigatitan often had bright colors along the underside of their neck and their body but their spines were as close to white as possible to show they were in good health.  Females would remain all one color on their body however, their tails would be brightly colored. Unlike males, darker spines on females were preferred. Albino variants of either gender were actively avoided.
> Well before nesting seasons began, Garrigatitan would begin their slow trudge towards their preferred nesting areas and would spend time before the season arrived to pick out their preferred mate.  Females took great time and care in picking from possible candidates to make sure they were in proper health.  After mating, female Garrigatitan would lay their eggs in areas with high amounts of geo-thermal activity; hotsprings and dormant volcanoes were popular choices as they were the best option to keep their eggs warm. Females wouldn’t linger long after hatchlings arrived, only staying about a day or so to give a chance for each hatchling to fully break free from their eggs.  Afterward, hatchlings would be on their own as they were too small to properly join the herd. Hatchlings would use their instincts to find food and to shelter themselves from possible predators until they were old enough and big enough to join the remainder of the herd. 

-

**Optional Behavior**:
Garrigatitan often preferred a solitary lifestyle. They were known to become aggressive when sharing food with other herbivores. Even in pairs, Garrigatitan were condescending towards one another and would frequently fight over resources even if they were bountiful. They had a particular disdain towards the water, swimming as little as possible and even going as far as attempting to take cover from rain under cliff overhangs that were large enough to house such a creature. Despite their sour nature, they had a notable admiration towards small herbivores like the Dryosaurus and Psittacosaurus, these small creatures lifting their mood greatly within a herd.

> 6 Hours Passive Growth Time
> 5500 Combat Weight

**Lifestyle:**
    Terrestrial

**Group Limit:** 
    3 adults, 5 offspring (below sub-adult)

**Herd Tolerance:**
> Albertaceratops, Camptosaurus, Halszkaraptor, Nasutoceratops, Pachycephalosaurus, Pentaceratops, Psittacosaurus, Struthiomimus, and Styracosaurus.
> *Please see the [Pack & Herd Rules](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1Xw4QL-RWispl_cPW3qCjBFKPsjfrNSfSqHjCJ3WWsP8/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.evs5v6kmvhm) for mix-herd limits.*

**Mandatory Behavior:** 
○ __Shield Wall__: When in a **ceratopsian herd** with adolescent and below present, adult Eotriceratops **must not** flee from combat unless gravely injured and death is likely [~15% hp or less].
○ __Territorial__: When in a ceratopsian herd with **2 or more adult Eotriceratops** present, adult Eotriceratops **must** be **vocally aggressive** to other adult apex ceratopsians **not** in their group.  If after 3 threat calls, the herbivore(s) do not attempt to flee, adult Eotriceratops *may* become **combat aggressive** and pursue the herbivore(s) until they flee the territory or perish. If fearful or unwilling to engage other adult apex ceratopsians, they **must immediately** leave the territory.
○ __Survivor__: Adolescent and below Eotriceratops **must flee** from adult sub-apex and above carnivores. May defend themself if cornered or exhausted.
-

**Ceratopsian Combat:**
Ceratopsian herds (those made up of Albertaceratops, Eotriceratops, Nasutoceratops, Pachyrhinosaurus, Pentaceratops, or Styracosaurus) often engage in herbi-on-herbi combat with each other, and may initiate combat __when specifically outlined in their profile mandatory behaviors.__  ﻿Ceratopsians may defend their herd from other ceratopsians.
- Forager-tier herbivores grouped with __only__ Ceratopsians in a mixed herd are still classified as a Ceratopsian Herd. When forager-tier herbivores, such as Camptosaurus, Dryosaurus,  Psittacosaurus, and Struthiomimus are in those herds, the forager-tier dinosaurs **must not** engage in Ceratopsian Herd vs Ceratopsian Herd combat. Instead, the forager-tiers **must flee** from this specific Ceratopsian combat.
- If a Ceratopsian(s) is in a mixed-herd with any low-tier and above herbivore that is not a Ceratopsian(s), they are **not** classified as a Ceratopsian Herd. [*See Pack & Herd Rules 6.(C)(i)*](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1Xw4QL-RWispl_cPW3qCjBFKPsjfrNSfSqHjCJ3WWsP8/edit?usp=sharing)
-

**Carnivore Aggression:**
- When **solo** or **adolescent and below** Eotriceratops are present, adult Eotriceratops may be **vocally aggressive** to all **approaching** carnivores. If after 3 threat calls the carnivores do not attempt to flee from the Eotriceratops and its herd, Eotriceratops may become combat aggressive and pursue the fleeing carnivore until they leave the POI or perish.
- When in an **adult only herd**, adult Eotriceratops may be **vocally aggressive** to **approaching** sub-apex and above carnivores. If after 3 threat calls the carnivores do not attempt to flee from the Eotriceratops and its herd, Eotriceratops may become combat aggressive and pursue the fleeing carnivore until they leave the POI or perish.
-

**Scavenger Aggression:** 
- When **solo** or **adolescent and below** are present, Eotriceratops are vocally aggressive towards grouped scavengers. If after 3 threat calls the scavengers do not attempt to flee from the Eotriceratops and its herd, Eotriceratops may become combat aggressive until they flee from the herd.
- When in an **adult-only herd**, Eotriceratops are unbothered by scavengers.

**Scavenger High Pounce/Latch Aggression**
- When pounced on by scavengers, they must be **bothered** and must immediately shake-off and **initiate combat** on the latched scavengers. In this scenario, if the scavengers cannot hunt this tier of dinosaur they **must not** fight back and instead **must flee**.
*Note: The latched on scavengers **must not** initiate combat first if it would break their profile's hunting restrictions/behaviors.*

**Territory Size:**
> Nomadic, cannot claim territories when solo or in an Eotriceratops only herd. [*Please see Territory Rules for mix-herd options.*](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1mnbJnMb6H9FuCYLmMM28ncJT-Ar6sIWk4qCUa89VwgI/edit?usp=sharing)

**Leadership Type:**
  Leading Bull - Male Dominant.
-

**Optional Nesting Behavior:**
- __Season__: Wet
- __Preferred Nesting Location(s)__: 
> **Gondwa**: Ripple Beach, Young Grove, Green Valley
> **Panjura**: Grassland Lake, Two Falls Hollow, Little Brook Meadow
- __Preferred Skins/Coloration(s)__: Males adorned themselves in bright, eye-catching colors across their entire body, while females matched males in similar patterns, if interested.
> During the wet season, male Eotriceratops took their time to sharpen their horns against the damp rocks as they traveled to their nesting grounds, where they would later participate in hostile fights against other bulls to claim the rights to nest that season with a potential female. Female Eotriceratop, in turn, traveled to these grounds and watched the combat between bulls for a potential partner.  If impressed by a bulls' combat prowess and strength, the female approached them.  They tended to prefer those males who were seasoned fighters, which was made clear by scarring and wear on male’s hides.
-

**Optional Behavior:** 
Eotriceratops form small groups, often consisting of one male and multiple females - such herding behavior comes with their aggressive and grumpy nature, which may lead to fights over resources or even favorite resting spots.  The herd has a hierarchical structure, which is led by a dominant male or a female, depending on their group composition.  Eotriceratops rarely seek help from other herbivorous species, instead relying only on each other due to their massive size and power and may bully smaller herbivores, pushing them away from food or shaded rest areas.  These herbivores are nomadic, which keeps them from overgrazing a single area, as their large group sizes and seemingly bottomless stomachs might easily lead to extinction of local flora if their wanderlust did not keep them moving to new areas.  Eotriceratops are strictly diurnal and will not move from shelter or forested areas during the night as their eyesight is much worse than carnivores that could potentially hunt them in the open plains.

> *Official Eotriceratops and WoTG’s Eotriceratops TLC share the same profile.*
> *Official Eotriceratops and WoTG’s Eotriceratops TLC share the same herd tolerances and may herd together.*

-

Edmontosaurus must not pursue fleeing scavengers.

**Scavenger Low Pounce/Latch Aggression**

In this scenario, if the scavengers cannot hunt this tier of dinosaur they **must not** fight back and instead **must flee**.

-

**Optional Nesting Behavior:**

> **Panjura**: Bower Brook Lake, Swamp Reservoir, Travelers Basin

> Though Edmontosaurus could be described as “lazy,” due to their large size, they were notably active during mating seasons.  Bulls would become territorial, fighting with one another to mate with females and to claim nesting grounds. These fights wouldn’t last very long however, as their overall lack of endurance made it so most fights were verbal rather than physical.  Despite their arrogance, male Edmontosaurus were notable caregivers to their young and their mates.  Hatchlings and juveniles would mimic the adults territorial behavior by play-fighting with one another. However,  having greater stamina their guardians, these training fights lasted quite a bit longer.

**Optional Behavior:** 
  Male Edmontosaurus tend to stay at the rear of the group while traveling, protecting the rear of the herd while the female Edmontosaurus leads.  Inactive at night, they often rest in a large circle formation with their young in the center.  Each adult will switch shifts guarding their herd throughout the rest period.  Although nomadic and migratory in nature, Edmontosaurus tend to stay at one water source for days at a time until food resources become scarce.  Being as large as they are, Edmontosaurus will try to conserve as much energy as they can, and so they will avoid sprinting if possible and take many grazing breaks through their travels.

> Deinocheirus weren’t just careful planners during nesting season, they were deeply attentive to their partners as well. Males and females performed graceful courtship dances in shallow water, their synchronized movements strengthening the bond between them. Even after the eggs were laid, pairs remained loyal, choosing to stay together through multiple seasons as they raised their young and guided them into independence. Female Deinocheirus were especially protective, often preening both their offspring and their mates as a gentle display of affection.

**Optional Behavior: **
Usually indifferent to most dinosaurs, Deinocheirus nonetheless migrate from one water source to another until they find a location worthy of raising their young. They prefer to travel during rainy weather, but won’t hesitate to abandon a site if they sense danger nearby. Female Deinocheirus are fiercely protective, aggressively driving off any unrelated males or any dinosaur bold enough to approach, the moment they enter the pair’s claimed waters. It’s common to see them along the lake or pond edge, sharpening their vigilance as they preen their feathers and guard what’s theirs.

**Mandatory Behavior:**
○ __Territorial__: When in their **claimed territory**, adult Barsboldia *may* be **vocally aggressive** to all adult sub-apex and above hadrosaurs. After 3 threat-calls and/or mock attacks, if the hadrosaurs do not attempt to move away or flee from the Barsboldia or its herd, Barsboldia *may* become **combat aggressive** until the hadrosaurs flee the territory or perish. 
- *Note: Sub-apex and above Hadrosaurs include: Barbsoldia, Edmontosaurus Annectans, Edmontosaurus Regalis, Iguanodon, and Parasaurolophus* 
○ __Survivor__: **Adolescent and below** Barsboldia, when **adult herbivores** are present, **must not** participate in combat between adult dinosaurs and **must attempt to flee** unless they are the main target.
○ __Rivalry__: **Adolescent and below** Barsboldia may claim **one adult** herbivore within their group. If another **adolescent or below** herbivore approaches their *claimed adult*, adolescent and below Barsboldia *may* threat-call or mock attack towards the adolescent or below herbivore. If after 3 threat calls/mock attacks, the adolescent or below herbivore does not move away from the claimed adult, adolescent and below Barsboldia may become **combat aggressive** until the other herbivores flee or perish.
-

**Carnivore Aggression:**
- When **solo** or **2 adult Barbolsdia**, Barsboldia **must** be **vocally aggressive** towards adult sub-apex and below carnivores. After **3 threat calls**, if the carnivores do not attempt to move away or flee from the Barsboldia or its herd, Barsboldia *may* become **combat aggressive** until the carnivores flee the territory or perish.
- When in a herd of **3 or more adult Barsboldia**, Barsboldia are unbothered by mid-tier and below carnivores, unless there is an attempt to initiate combat by the carnivores’ attacks on any herd member.  
- When in a **mix-herd**, or **adolescent and below hadrosaurs** are present, adult Barsboldia **must** be **vocally aggressive** to all adult carnivores.  If after 3 threat calls a carnivores does not attempt to move away or flee from the Barsboldia or its herd, **adult Barsboldia** may become **combat aggressive** until the carnivores flee the territory or perish. 

**Scavenger Aggression: **
- When **solo** adult or in an **adult-only herd**, Barsboldia do not perceive scavengers as a potential threat.  
- When **adolsecent and below hadrosaurs** are present, adult Barsboldia must be **vocally aggressive** towards scavenger *groups*. If after 3 threat calls the scavengers do not attempt to flee from Barsboldia and its herd, Barsboldia *may* be **combat aggressive** until the scavengers flee from its herd or perish.
-

**Scavenger Low Pounce/Latch Aggression**
- When pounced on by scavengers, may be unbothered. If bothered by the latched on scavengers, they must threat call **three** times or shake **three** times before attacking the scavengers. In this scenario, if the scavengers cannot hunt this tier of dinosaur they **must not** fight back and instead **must flee**.
*Note: The latched on scavengers **must not** initiate combat first if it would break their profile's hunting restrictions/behaviors.*

**Territory Size**
> Large - Can claim as a solo adult or Barsboldia group. [*Please see Territory Rules for mix-herd options.*](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1mnbJnMb6H9FuCYLmMM28ncJT-Ar6sIWk4qCUa89VwgI/edit?usp=sharing)

**Leadership Type:**
    Leading Bull - Male Dominant.
-

**Optional Nesting Behavior:**
- __Season__: Wet
- __Preferred Nesting Location(s)__: 
> **Gondwa**: Birchwoods, Green Valley, Triad Falls
> **Panjura**: Grassland Lake, Riverland, Threehorn Grassland 
- __Preferred Skins/Coloration(s)__: Outside nesting season, both genders were found marked with dull and dark colors.  During nesting and courtship season, males displayed vibrant color patches across their entire body.  Albino and white colorations were highly discouraged and may be avoided.
> Though docile throughout the year, males would experience a rare temperament change during nesting seasons.  They became aggressive towards other bulls within the herd, butting heads often and picking fights with one another attempting to be seen as a more viable candidate by the females of the herd. Once the eggs had hatched, these new fathers became very protective towards their young and their mate that was now more vulnerable.  Males adorned themselves in bright colors during nesting seasons in an attempt to stand out more, often showing off in large fields and having ritualistic fights.  Victors of these fights would get to mate but the unfortunate loser could potentially face exile from the herd. 

**Optional Behavior: **
  Bull Barsboldia tend to be more vocal amongst themselves than their female counterparts, creating miniature bachelor groups within the herd while cows will watch over and graze with their young.  During the harsh sunlight hours, Barsboldia groups will congregate underneath the shade of trees or bathe in nearby streams to stay cool.  Bulls will battle for dominance over the herd, sometimes exiling the losing bull.  When confronted by a carnivore, cows will put their young first, often matching their pace while a singular bull attempts to lead the carnivore away.

﻿
**Mandatory Behaviour**:
○ __Survivor:__ When **Adolescent or below**, Apatosaurus avoids participating in combat with** Apex tier carnivores**, unless attacked first.

○ __Aquaphobic__:  Apatosaurus **must not** enter the water once combat has started.

**Carnivore Aggression**:

In this scenario, if the scavengers cannot hunt this tier of dinosaur they **must not** fight back and instead **must flee**.
*Note: The latched on scavengers **must not** initiate combat first if it would break their profile's hunting restrictions/behaviors.*
-

**Territory Size**:

**Leadership Type**:
    Independent  -  Will prioritize their own personal survival and benefit before all others.

**Optional Nesting Behavior**:
- __Season__:  Wet
- __Preferred Nesting Location(s)__:
> **Gondwa**: Burned Forest, Hotsprings, Triad Falls
> **Panjura**: Grassland Crater, Lake Pleasant, World’s Edge Falls
> **Riparia**: Black Fern Hills, Redwoods, Tallbrush Coasts
- __Preferred Skin(s) Coloration(s)__: Males would sometimes shed their coats while traveling to nesting locations to match the environment they chose.  Their heads, necks, and spine would have brighter shades while the remainder of their body would stay one consistent tone.  This was a rare sight for males as they usually felt indifferent towards the nesting season as a whole.  Females would remain the same shades with little to no variations, but, on rare occasions, they may attempt to have similar shades to their partner.
> Generally, Apatosaurus didn’t take much time or put much care into nesting as they did not want to bear the responsibility of offspring that struggled to keep up with the herd.  On rare occasions, when two would bond, they would mate for life - not only for the comfort of having a partner but also having someone to assist in battle when the time came.  Females would travel to open areas with large bodies of water or areas with heated pockets of land to keep eggs warm.  Maternal instincts were strong for female Apatosaurus if they bonded with their young and they would carefully build their nests and take their time to bring them branches from berry bushes, so hatchlings wouldn’t have to leave until they were practiced enough to walk without stumbling over their large feet.  Offspring would play amongst themselves and chase their parents' tails, only to tire themselves out and use them as pillows to take their naps.

Optional Behavior:
     The Apatosaurus, despite sharing the appearance of the notably docile Diplodocus, were known for their hot-headed temperament toward not only carnivores, but other herbivores as well. Though generally selfish, within herds with Amargasaurus, they became incredibly defensive and would protect the smaller sauropod with their lives, if necessary.  Apatosaurus were very expressive creatures, especially while migrating with their herds; they would use both verbal and non-verbal forms of communication to lead herdmates towards food and water.  When herded with other sauropods and other small herbivores, like Camptosaurus and Dryosaurus, their tones would shift and they’d even allow these creatures to eat and drink before they did.  This same behavior wouldn’t transfer to other larger packmates however.

They will mostly stick to forests as much as possible for food and water sources, avoiding the open and flat sands of the desert and the rocky terrains of the canyons, especially when deciding where to sleep for the night.

Due to their small size, hatchling Amargasaurus chose to stay inside the nests until reaching their juvenile growth stage. However, males would have a vibrant dome color that was unlike the rest of their body. Younger males would often have more vibrant colors and, as they would age, the color would soften.
> Pachycephalosaurus would go through a plethora of rituals during nesting seasons.  Known for their energy and vigor, males would compete amongst one another to show their strength and endurance especially with the harsh environments they’d reside in.  At times, males would quite literally butt heads, oftening fighting against one another by ramming their domes in an attempt to overpower one another.  This approach was only common with hot-headed and younger males, while older males would prefer to do courting dances with their mate, not being as energetic as they once were.  The older males were also more emotionally in-tune with their mate.  Both males and females would take turns foraging for food and teaching their young how to use their dome to their advantage when foraging.  Hatchlings and juveniles were typically very rowdy, though the runts of the litter were more docile.  Female’s would tend to the runts more closely, becoming very protective of them and making sure they got to eat first.
-

**Optional Behavior:** 
  When it comes to who to follow, the most dominant male is chosen by sparring contests.  Females, although not the most caring parents, will keep their young close until they reach sub-adult.  They are social, often being vocal to communicate with the herd.  Males frequently spar, often to decide dominance or sometimes even over food.

This same behavior translated into their parenting behavior. **Ceratopsian Combat:**
Ceratopsian herds (those made up of Albertaceratops, Eotriceratops, Nasutoceratops, Pachyrhinosaurus, Pentaceratops, or Styracosaurus) often engage in herbi-on-herbi combat with each other, and may initiate combat __when specifically outlined in their profile mandatory behaviors.__  ﻿Ceratopsians may defend their herd from other ceratopsians.
- Forager-tier herbivores grouped with __only__ Ceratopsians in a mixed herd are still classified as a Ceratopsian Herd. When forager-tier herbivores, such as Camptosaurus, Halszkaraptor, Psittacosaurus, and Struthiomimus are in those herds, the forager-tier dinosaurs **must not** engage in Ceratopsian Herd vs Ceratopsian Herd combat. - **Solo** Psittacosaurus of any age will **avoid** confrontation with **solo** adult low-tier carnivores, but may defend themselves if there is an attempt to initiate combat. 
- When **in a herd**, Psittacosaurus may be **vocal** when they sight any scavenger, or above-listed carnivores, no matter their size or age, to alert the rest of the herd. > Psittacosaurus were highly social creatures and made exceptional parental figures to their young.  Females took time and care into gathering small feathers, soft leaves, and dirt, which they placed in a pile to create nests.  While some preferred to lay eggs in intricate tunnel systems underground, many psittaco’s preferred to have large communal nesting areas shared with Citipati, where elders would take turns caring for other members’ kin, which left others free to go on outings to find food.  Juvenile psittacosaurus were inquisitive and mischievous, sometimes play-fighting with siblings, and oftentimes staying outside too long when it became dark.  On occasion, Citipati were kind enough to round up any straggling young and return them to their nests.
-

**Optional Behavior:**
  Psittacosaurus exhibit a unique and remarkable social structure.  These creatures, adorned with feather-like quills in a vivid array of colors, demonstrate a collective sense of empathy, intelligence, and effective communication through distinct vocalizations.  Psittacosaurus family units are characterized by cooperative behavior, with adults actively engaging in the collection of a diverse range of nuts, flowers, and berries.  This provisioning is directed towards both the young members and the elderly or injured individuals within the group.  The creatures showcase a notable level of ingenuity in navigating their environment, employing problem-solving skills to optimize foraging routes and circumvent potential threats.  Among the males, a social hierarchy is established through elaborate displays of color and vocal prowess.  Leadership over family units is quite often contested, leading to confrontations between males, particularly conflicts arising between biological sons and their fathers.  During the nesting season, Psittacosaurus exhibit a pattern of forming weak pair bonds, and with each subsequent season, mates are changed.  This cyclic arrangement facilitates genetic diversity within family units.  The young Psittacosaurus, marked by their exuberance and curiosity, often engage in playful activities within the family setting.  Their vocalizations take the form of games and mock battles, occasionally leading to interactions with adult members.  However, their curiosity, while endearing, exposes them to potential risks such as predation, becoming lost, or inadvertently ingesting harmful plants.

**Mandatory Behavior:**
○ __Territorial__:  When at 6 adults and in their **claimed pond**, adult Halszkaraptor must be vocally aggressive to other adult Halszkaraptors not in their group.  If after 3 threat calls the other Halszkaraptor do not flee, Halszkaraptor *may* become combat aggressive until the other Halszkaraptor flee from the claimed pond.
○ __Fearful Abandonment__:  Halszkaraptor are **fearful of** and **must flee** from adult mid-tier and above carnivores.  May defend themself if cornered or exhausted.
○ __Survivor__:  Adolescent and below Halszkaraptor **must flee** from all adult carnivores.  May defend themself if cornered or exhauted.
-

**Ceratopsian Combat:**
Ceratopsian herds (those made up of Albertaceratops, Eotriceratops, Nasutoceratops, Pachyrhinosaurus, Pentaceratops, or Styracosaurus) often engage in herbi-on-herbi combat with each other, and may initiate combat __when specifically outlined in their profile mandatory behaviors.__  ﻿Ceratopsians may defend their herd from other ceratopsians.
- Forager-tier herbivores grouped with __only__ Ceratopsians in a mixed herd are still classified as a Ceratopsian Herd. When forager-tier herbivores, such as Camptosaurus, Halszkaraptor, Psittacosaurus, and Struthiomimus are in those herds, the forager-tier dinosaurs **must not** engage in Ceratopsian Herd vs Ceratopsian Herd combat. Instead, the forager-tiers **must flee** from this specific Ceratopsian combat.
- If a Ceratopsian(s) is in a mixed-herd with any low-tier and above herbivore that is not a Ceratopsian(s), they are **not** classified as a Ceratopsian Herd. [*See Pack & Herd Rules 6.(C)(i)*](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1Xw4QL-RWispl_cPW3qCjBFKPsjfrNSfSqHjCJ3WWsP8/edit?usp=sharing)
-

**Carnivore Aggression:**
- When in a Halszkaraptor-only group, adult Halszkaraptor **must be** vocally aggressive towards all unattended juvenile and below carnivores attempting to drink from their pond.  If after 3 threat calls the carnivores do not attempt to flee, Halszkaraptor *may* be combat aggressive until the carnivores flee from the pond.
- When in a mixed herd with adult sub-apex and above herbivores present, adult Halszkaraptor **must be** vocally aggressive to approaching adult low-tier carnivores.  If after 3 threat calls the carnivores do not attempt to flee from Halszkaraptor and its herd, Halszkaraptor *may* become combat aggressive and pursue fleeing carnivores until they flee the POI or perish.
**Scavenger Aggression:**
- When in a group of 4 or more adult Halszkaraptor, they **must be** vocally aggressive towards *adult scavengers*.  After 3 threat calls, if the scavenger(s) do not attempt to flee from Halszkaraptor and its herd, adult Halszkaraptor *may* become combat aggressive and pursue fleeing scavengers until they flee the POI or perish.

**Scavenger High Pounce/Latch Aggression:**
- When pounced on by scavengers, must be **bothered** and must immediately shake-off and **initiate combat** on the latched scavengers. In this scenario, if the scavengers cannot hunt this tier of dinosaur they must **not fight** back and instead must **flee**.
*Note: The latched on scavengers cannot attack/initiate combat first if it would break their profile's hunting restrictions/behaviors.*

**Territory Size:**
       Only able to claim ponds - Needs 6 adult members to claim.

**Leadership Type:**
       Mated Pair - Dual Leadership
-

**Optional Nesting Behavior:**
- __Season__: Wet & Winter Season 
- __Preferred Nesting Location(s)__: 
**Gondwa**: Young Grove, White Cliffs Lake, Snake Gully
**Panjura**: The Crook, Little Stone Pond, Crater Pond
- __Preferred Skins/Coloration(s)__: Male Halszkaraptor usually become more colorful during nesting seasons to attract a mate. Female Halszkaraptor usually stick to more neutral colorations to blend in with the surrounding area to prevent predators from finding their nests.
*Description*
Bonding for life, Halszakaraptor will remain with their mate for multiple nesting seasons and prefer to nest on the shore of ponds and lakes. A young Halszkaraptor will follow their mother around wherever she may go, and in turn, mothers will watch over their nest and young until they reach adulthood. Once a mother's male younglings reach sub-adulthood, the leading male Halszkaraptor will often chase out these fresh sub-adult males. The male leader will take on the role of protecting the entire group and often partakes in fighting other males for dominance and to win over other female Halszkaraptor during nesting seasons.
-

**Optional Behavior:**
Adult male Halszkaraptors are aggressive towards other male Halszkaraptor in order to protect it’s family against any potential threats.  Halszkaraptor love to swim in bodies of water and young Halszkaraptor will often be seen playing with their parent(s) or other young in the water.  Halszkaraptor are socially oriented, often performing acts of courtship by bringing their mates or siblings gifts in rocks, fish, shells, or anything else found on the shores of their beaches.  Female Halszkaraptors will hang around each other for company and comfort, taking turns caring for young Halszkaraptor.  During this time, the leading females will correct a young Halszkaraptor’s behaviors, often seen redirecting or even pecking at them to make them stop. 
Young Halszkaraptor life consists of sticking close to their mothers and other young Halszkaraptors. They play in groups and are not often alone. They display different behaviors and personalities from youngling to youngling, however, the most common behavior is crying for their parent(s); often in an attempt for attention when hungry, distressed, or even just for fun.

**Scavenger Aggression:**

**Scavenger High Pounce/Latch Aggression**

> Citipati formed social nesting groups amongst themselves, and were even found sharing nesting grounds with  Psittacosaurus.  Males spent the entire nesting season brooding and raising chicks whereas the females foraged for food and led predators away from the nesting site.  When the chicks reached adolescence, they began to venture out of the nest and join the females in foraging for food.

> *When grouped with Psittacosauri, male Citipati will also carefully guard Psittaco offspring. When night falls, and females return to the nest to rest, the males would leave the nest to help gather any stray Psittaco offspring back to the nesting grounds.*
-

**Optional Behavior:**
   Citipati are natural caregivers; caring for their sick and hurt herd members or protecting their young under their plumage.  They enjoy vocalizing during the daylight hours, socializing by singing songs to each other or when looking for other lost members of their herd.  In the evenings, Citipati will splash and bathe in the cool waters of a nearby water source, cleaning their feathers before resting for the night.  Young Citipati tend to be curious and when physically able to leave the nest, will often wander off to explore, requiring their parents to search and carry them back to the nest.  Males will often adorn striking colors, dancing and flashing their vibrant plumage at females in an attempt of courtship; females will sync their dances if they accept.

**Group Limit:**
    4 adults, 4 Offspring (Below sub-adult)

**Herd Tolerance:** 
>   Albertaceratops, Amargasaurus, Apatosaurus, Barsboldia, Bonapartenykus, Camptosaurus, Citipati, Edmontosaurus Annectens, Edmontosaurus Regalis, Garrigatitan, Gigantoraptor, Halszkaraptor, Iguanodon, Lambeosaurus, Lurdusaurus, Nasutoceratops, Pachyrhinosaurus, Parasaurolophus, Pentaceratops, Struthiomimus, Tenontosaurus, and Yunnanosaurus.  
> *Please see the [Pack & Herd Rules](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1Xw4QL-RWispl_cPW3qCjBFKPsjfrNSfSqHjCJ3WWsP8/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.evs5v6kmvhm) for mix-herd limits.*

**Mandatory Behavior:**
○ __Pacifist__: When in an **adult-only** herd, Muttaburrasaurus **must not** defend berry bushes from other herbivores. If threat-called by another herbivore while within one Argentinosaurus-length of a berry bush, Muttaburrasaurus must **immediately** move away from the berry bush. 
○ __Protective Parent__: When **adolescent and below** are present, adult Muttaburrasaurus *may* be **vocally aggressive** towards *approaching* adult low tier up to adult sub-apex herbivores. If after 3 threat-calls and/or mock-attacks, the adult herbivores not in their group do not attempt to move away or flee from Muttaburrasaurus and its herd, adult Muttaburrasaurus may become **combat aggressive** until the herbivores flee or perish. 
○ __Survivor__: **Adolescent and below** Muttaburrasaurus **must flee** from adult low tier and above carnivores. May defend themself if cornered or exhausted.
-

**Carnivore Aggression:**
- When **solo**, Muttaburrasaurus **must flee** from adult sub-apex and above carnivores **unless** cornered or exhausted [~15% Stamina]. If Muttaburrasaurus successfully defends themself, they **must not chase** fleeing carnivores.
- When in an **adult-only herd**, Muttaburrasaurus *may* be **vocally aggressive** towards adult mid-tier and below carnivores. If after **3 threat calls**, the carnivores do not attempt to move away or flee from the Muttaburrasaurus or its herd,  they *may* become **combat aggressive** until the carnivores flee the territory or perish.
- When in a herd with **adolescent and below** herbivores present, adult Muttaburrasaurus *may* be **vocally aggressive** towards adult sub-apex and below carnivores. If after **3 threat calls**, the carnivores do not attempt to move away or flee from the Muttaburrasaurus and its herd, they *may* become **combat aggressive** until the carnivores flee the territory or perish.
-

**Scavenger Aggression:**
- In an adult-only herd, Muttaburrasaurus do not perceive scavengers as a threat.  
- When **solo** or with **adolescent and below** herbivores present, Muttaburrasaurus **must** be **vocally aggressive** to scavenger tier carnivores.  If after 3 threat calls or 3 mock attacks, the scavengers do not attempt to move away or flee from the Muttaburrasaurus and its herd, they *may* become **combat aggressive** until the scavengers flee the territory or perish. 

**Scavenger Low Pounce/Latch Aggression**
- When pounced on by scavengers, may be unbothered. If bothered by the latched on scavengers, they must threat call **three** times or shake **three** times before attacking the scavengers. In this scenario, if the scavengers cannot hunt this tier of dinosaur they **must not** fight back and instead **must flee**.
*Note: The latched on scavengers **must not** initiate combat first if it would break their profile's hunting restrictions/behaviors.*
-

**Territory Size:**
> Small - Needs 3 adult members to claim 
> Medium - Needs 4 adult members to claim 
> [*Please see Territory Rules for mix-herd options.*](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1mnbJnMb6H9FuCYLmMM28ncJT-Ar6sIWk4qCUa89VwgI/edit?usp=sharing)

**Leadership Type:**
   Matriarchal - Female Dominant.  

**Optional Nesting Behavior:**
- __Season__: Dry  
- __Preferred Nesting Location(s)__: 
> **Gondwa**:  Burned Forest, Rainbow Hills, Rockfall hill 
> **Panjura**: Amphitheater Lake, Aperture Pass, Pleasant Hill Haven
> **Riparia:** Black Fern Hill, Coastland Swamp, Tallbrush Coast
- __Preferred Skins/Coloration(s)__:  
> Female Muttburrasaurus are typically Duller in color, often soft earthy tones to better conceal them when caring for young or away from predators.
> Male Muttaburrasaurus also carry dull tones blending in well with their environment until mating season when they will acquire flushes of brighter color to help with courting the females with the group. 
-
**Optional Behavior:**
Hidden around freshwater sources and tall reeds, Muttaburrasaurus gather in relaxed, social herds. They wade through shallow ponds to keep cool and protect their skin from the sun, often interacting calmly with nearby herbivores. Rarely territorial, they willingly give up food, offering soft, submissive calls before returning to their leisurely routines.

They show gentle curiosity toward other species, exchanging quiet grunts or chirps as if greeting them. Young individuals splash and play in the shallows while adults rest along the banks, blowing bubbles or soaking peacefully. As dusk falls, their low, resonant calls drift through the reeds, adding a soothing sense of calm to the wetlands they call home.
``` ```

``` ```
> 3 Hours Passive Growth Time
> 3500 Combat Weight

**Lifestyle:**
    Terrestrial

**Group Limit:**
    6 adults, 6 offspring (below sub-adult) 

**Herd Tolerance:**
   Amargasaurus, Eotriceratops, Iguanodon, Nasutoceratops, Pachycephalosaurus, Pachyrhinosaurus, Pentaceratops, Psittacosaurus, Struthiomimus, and Yunnanosaurus.
*Please see the [Pack & Herd Rules](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1Xw4QL-RWispl_cPW3qCjBFKPsjfrNSfSqHjCJ3WWsP8/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.evs5v6kmvhm) for mix-herd limits.*

**Mandatory Behavior:**
○ __Nemesis__: When in a ceratopsian herd,  Albertaceratops must be **vocally aggressive** to approaching adult mid-tier and below ceratopsian herds. After 3 threat calls, if the adult mid-tier and/or below ceratopsians do not flee from Albertaceratops and its herd, Albertaceratops may become **combat aggressive** and chase the ceratopsian(s) out of the POI.
○ __Territorial__: When in an Albertacertops-only herd's **claimed territory**, adult Albertaceratops must be vocally aggressive to other adult Albertaceratops not in their group.  If after 3 threat calls, the herbivore(s) do not attempt to flee, adult Albertaceratops may become combat aggressive and chase the herbivore(s) out of the POI. If fearful or unwilling to engage other adult Albertaceratops, they must immediately surrender the territory and leave the POI.
○ __Survivor__: Adolescent and below Albertaceratops may defend themselves from adolescent and below carnivores and adult low-tier carnivores.  They must flee from adult mid-tier and above carnivores unless cornered or exhausted.

-

**Ceratopsian Combat:**
Ceratopsian herds (those made up of Albertaceratops, Eotriceratops, Nasutoceratops, Pachyrhinosaurus, Pentaceratops, or Styracosaurus) often engage in herbi-on-herbi combat with each other, and may initiate combat __when specifically outlined in their profile mandatory behaviors.__  ﻿Ceratopsians may defend their herd from other ceratopsians.
- Forager-tier herbivores grouped with __only__ Ceratopsians in a mixed herd are still classified as a Ceratopsian Herd. When forager-tier herbivores, such as Camptosaurus, Halszkaraptor, Psittacosaurus, and Struthiomimus are in those herds, the forager-tier dinosaurs **must not** engage in Ceratopsian Herd vs Ceratopsian Herd combat. Instead, the forager-tiers **must flee** from this specific Ceratopsian combat.
- If a Ceratopsian(s) is in a mixed-herd with any low-tier and above herbivore that is not a Ceratopsian(s), they are **not** classified as a Ceratopsian Herd. [*See Pack & Herd Rules 6.(C)(i)*](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1Xw4QL-RWispl_cPW3qCjBFKPsjfrNSfSqHjCJ3WWsP8/edit?usp=sharing)
-

**Carnivore Aggression:**
- Adult Albertaceratops are **vocally aggressive** to adult mid-tier carnivores.  If after 3 threat calls the carnivores do not attempt to flee away from the Albertaceratops, the Albertaceratops may become combat aggressive and chase the carnivore(s) out of the POI.
- When adolescent or below are present,  adult Albertaceratops are **vocally aggressive** to *approaching* adult low-tier carnivores.  If after 3 threat calls, the carnivores do not attempt to flee from Albertaceratops and its herd, Albertaceratops may become combat aggressive and chase the carnivore(s) out of the POI.

**Scavenger Aggression:**
- Adult Albertaceratops are unbothered by solo scavengers. 
- Adult Albertaceratops are vocally aggressive to grouped scavengers. 
- When with adolescent and below, Albertaceratops are vocally aggressive to all scavengers' no matter the scavs group size.

**Scavenger Moderate Pounce/Latch Aggression**
- When pounced on by scavengers, may be unbothered. If bothered by the latched on scavengers, must threat call **two** times or shake **two** times before attacking the scavengers. In this scenario, if the scavengers cannot hunt this tier of dinosaur they must **not fight** back and instead must **flee**.
*Note: The latched on scavengers cannot attack/initiate combat first if it would break their profile's hunting restrictions/behaviors.*

-

**Territory Size:**
  Small - When in an Albertaceratops only herd, needs at least 3 adult members to claim. [*Please see Territory Rules for mix-herd options.*](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1mnbJnMb6H9FuCYLmMM28ncJT-Ar6sIWk4qCUa89VwgI/edit?usp=sharing)

**Leadership Type:**
  Patriarchal - Male Dominant. 

**Optional Nesting Behavior:**
- __Season__: Dry
- __Preferred Nesting Location(s)__: 
> **Gondwa**: Green Valley, Green Hills, Grand Plains
> **Panjura**: Deep Pond, Grassland Lake
- __Preferred Skins/Coloration(s)__: Females will seek out mates that have coloration similar to their environment and the more scars a male has, the more likely they are to be selected.
> Albertaceratops will migrate to their nesting grounds in large groups, where the females will form open circles for males to fight.  Being an important process, these duels decide who stays in the herd and who leaves.  If a bull falls under 25% health, they must concede and leave the nesting grounds with hopes of finding mates elsewhere.  Females seek out only the strongest males and lesser bulls are weeded out until there are only two dominant bulls remaining to take care of the harem.  When in their nesting grounds, Albertaceratops may follow their __Territorial__ behavior, becoming highly intolerant to other ungrouped Albertaceratops and low tier herbivores eating their food.
-

**Optional Behavior:**
  Albertaceratops tend to be migratory in the morning and evenings until they find a suitable location to raise their young.  The needs of the young are always put first, so young move in the middle or front of the herd.  If there is harsh weather, they will hunker down under the best shelter they can find and form a sleeping circle, keeping the young center to lessen their blow from the unruly weather.  Preferring drier climates, Albertaceratops will move between water sources often and always search for food.  The bulls tend to be more aggressive towards other bulls, especially during mating seasons, and often spar with each other to challenge their strength.  Females will choose to partner with the strongest bulls and those with the most scars.
